vector<int> findSubstring(string s, vector<string>& words) {

   int m = words.size();

   if (m == 0) return {};

   int word_length = words[0].length();

   int total_length = m * word_length;

   int n = s.length();

   if (total_length > n) return {};

   // Create frequency map

   unordered_map<string, int> freq;

   for (auto& word : words) {

      freq[word]++;

   }

   vector<int> result;

   for (int i = 0; i <= n - total_length; ++i) {

      // Create a copy of freq for this i

      auto current = freq;

      bool valid = true;

      for (int k = 0; k < m; ++k) {

         string sub = s.substr(i + k*word_length, word_length);

         if (current[sub] == 0 || current.find(sub) == current.end()) {

            valid = false;

            break;

         }

         current[sub]--;

      }

      if (valid) {

         result.push_back(i);

      }

   }

   return result;

}

So, the precomputed substrings would be for each position j in s where j + word_length <= s.length(), substrings[j] = s.substr(j, word_length).

Then, for a window starting at i, the words are substrings[i], substrings[i + word_length], substrings[i + 2*word_length], ..., substrings[i + (m-1)*word_length].

This would save time in the inner loop, as accessing substrings[j] is O(1) instead of creating a new string each time.

So, the optimized code would look like:

vector<int> findSubstring(string s, vector<string>& words) {

   int m = words.size();

   if (m == 0) return {};

   int word_length = words[0].length();

   int total_length = m * word_length;

   int n = s.length();

   if (total_length > n) return {};

   // Precompute all possible word_length substrings

   vector<string> substrings;

   for (int i = 0; i <= n - word_length; ++i) {

      substrings.push_back(s.substr(i, word_length));

   }

   // Create frequency map

   unordered_map<string, int> freq;

   for (auto& word : words) {

      freq[word]++;

   }

   vector<int> result;

   for (int i = 0; i <= n - total_length; ++i) {

      auto current = freq;

      bool valid = true;

      for (int k = 0; k < m; ++k) {

         int pos = i + k * word_length;

         if (pos >= substrings.size()) {

            valid = false;

            break;

         }

         string word = substrings[pos];

         if (current[word] == 0 || current.find(word) == current.end()) {

            valid = false;

            break;

         }

         current[word]--;

      }

      if (valid) {

         result.push_back(i);

      }

   }

   return result;

}

This way, the substrings are precomputed, and accessing them is O(1) in the inner loop.

This should improve the performance.

Thus, the final code is as above.

solution:
```cpp
#include <vector>
#include <string>
#include <unordered_map>

using namespace std;

class Solution {
public:
    vector<int> findSubstring(string s, vector<string>& words) {
        int m = words.size();
        if (m == 0) return {};
        int word_length = words[0].length();
        int total_length = m * word_length;
        int n = s.length();
        if (total_length > n) return {};

        vector<string> substrings;
        for (int i = 0; i <= n - word_length; ++i) {
            substrings.push_back(s.substr(i, word_length));
        }

        unordered_map<string, int> freq;
        for (auto& word : words) {
            freq[word]++;
        }

        vector<int> result;
        for (int i = 0; i <= n - total_length; ++i) {
            auto current = freq;
            bool valid = true;
            for (int k = 0; k < m; ++k) {
                int pos = i + k * word_length;
                if (pos >= substrings.size()) {
                    valid = false;
                    break;
                }
                string word = substrings[pos];
                if (current[word] == 0 || current.find(word) == current.end()) {
                    valid = false;
                    break;
                }
                current[word]--;
            }
            if (valid) {
                result.push_back(i);
            }
        }
        return result;
    }
};
```

efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O((n - m * L + 1) * m * L)
space complexity: O(n * L + m)
energy implications: LOW

explanation: The solution efficiently checks each possible starting index in the string `s` by leveraging a sliding window approach combined with a frequency map to track word occurrences. Precomputing all possible substrings of the required word length optimizes the inner loop, ensuring quick access and reducing redundant computations. This approach ensures that each potential window is validated in linear time relative to the number of words, making it both time and space efficient for the given problem constraints.
